description: Common foods, drinks, and dining vocabulary

items.notes: General word for food. 'Ruokakauppa' = grocery store (lit: food shop)
examples: Ruoka on valmista. — The food is ready.

items.notes: A drink (noun). The verb 'to drink' is 'juoda'
examples: Haluatko juoman? — Would you like a drink?

items.notes: Finnish tap water is among the cleanest in the world and always safe to drink
examples: Saisinko lasin vettä? — Could I get a glass of water?

items.notes: Finland is the world's top coffee consumer per capita. 'Kahvitauko' = coffee break
examples: Juon kahvia joka aamu. — I drink coffee every morning.

items.notes: Less common than coffee in Finland but still widely enjoyed
examples: Haluatko kupillisen teetä? — Would you like a cup of tea?

items.notes: Finns drink a lot of milk, often with meals. 'Piimä' = buttermilk, also very popular
examples: Otatko maitoa kahviin? — Do you take milk in your coffee?

items.notes: General word for juice. 'Appelsiinimehu' = orange juice, 'omenamehu' = apple juice
examples: Lapset juovat mehua. — The children are drinking juice.

items.notes: Strong beer (over 4.7%) is only sold at Alko, the state alcohol monopoly stores
examples: Yksi olut, kiitos. — One beer, please.

items.notes: Wine is only sold at Alko stores in Finland. 'Punaviini' = red wine, 'valkoviini' = white wine
examples: Haluaisitko lasin viiniä? — Would you like a glass of wine?

items.notes: Finland is famous for dark rye bread ('ruisleipä'). Bread is a staple at every meal
examples: Ruisleipä on terveellistä. — Rye bread is healthy.

items.notes: Also means 'can' (verb) or is used as an exclamation: 'Voi ei!' = 'Oh no!'
examples: Laita voita leivän päälle. — Put butter on the bread.

items.notes: Finnish 'leipäjuusto' (bread cheese) is a unique squeaky cheese, often served with cloudberry jam
examples: Tämä juusto on hyvää. — This cheese is good.

items.notes: General word for meat. 'Naudanliha' = beef, 'sianliha' = pork, 'poronliha' = reindeer meat
examples: Syötkö lihaa? — Do you eat meat?

items.notes: Fish is very important in Finnish cuisine, with thousands of lakes providing fresh fish
examples: Kala on tuoretta. — The fish is fresh.

items.notes: Means both the animal and the meat. 'Kananmuna' = egg (lit: chicken egg)
examples: Syömme kanaa tänään. — We are eating chicken today.

items.notes: A staple of Finnish cuisine. 'Perunamuusi' = mashed potatoes, 'uudet perunat' = new potatoes (a summer delicacy)
examples: Perunat ovat valmiita. — The potatoes are ready.

items.notes: Used in Finnish 'riisipuuro' (rice porridge), a traditional Christmas dish
examples: Haluatko riisiä vai perunaa? — Would you like rice or potato?

items.notes: Same word as in English. 'Makaronilaatikko' (macaroni casserole) is a classic Finnish comfort food
examples: Teetkö pastaa tänään? — Are you making pasta today?

items.notes: Can refer to both salad and lettuce. Note the double 't'
examples: Otan salaatin, kiitos. — I'll have a salad, please.

items.notes: 'Hernekeitto' (pea soup) is traditionally eaten on Thursdays in Finland. 'Lohikeitto' = salmon soup
examples: Lohikeitto on herkullista. — Salmon soup is delicious.

items.notes: General word for fruit. Wild berries are more traditional in Finland than imported fruits
examples: Syön hedelmiä joka päivä. — I eat fruit every day.

items.notes: Apples grow in southern Finland. 'Omenapiirakka' = apple pie
examples: Tämä omena on makea. — This apple is sweet.

items.notes: One of the most popular imported fruits in Finland
examples: Osta banaaneja kaupasta. — Buy bananas from the store.

items.notes: Finnish summer strawberries are famous for their sweetness due to long daylight hours. 'Mansikkakakku' = strawberry cake
examples: Suomalaiset mansikat ovat makeita. — Finnish strawberries are sweet.

items.notes: Finnish forests are full of wild blueberries. 'Mustikkapiirakka' = blueberry pie. Everyman's right allows anyone to pick wild berries freely
examples: Keräsimme mustikoita metsästä. — We picked blueberries from the forest.

items.notes: Note the double 't'. Often used in salads and sauces
examples: Tomaatit ovat punaisia. — Tomatoes are red.

items.notes: A staple in Finnish cooking. 'Punasipuli' = red onion
examples: Pilko sipuli pieniksi paloiksi. — Chop the onion into small pieces.

items.notes: 'Porkkanalaatikko' (carrot casserole) is a traditional Finnish Christmas dish
examples: Kanit syövät porkkanoita. — Rabbits eat carrots.

items.notes: One of the most important fish in Finland. 'Lohikeitto' (salmon soup) and 'graavilohi' (cured salmon) are national favorites
examples: Lohi on Suomen suosituin kala. — Salmon is Finland's most popular fish.

items.notes: Finns love grilling sausages, especially at the summer cottage ('mökki'). 'Grillimakkara' = grilling sausage
examples: Grillataan makkaraa! — Let's grill sausages!

items.notes: Iconic Finnish cardamom-flavored sweet bread, served with coffee. 'Korvapuusti' = cinnamon roll (a type of pulla)
examples: Pulla tuoksuu ihanalta. — The sweet bread smells wonderful.

items.notes: 'Täytekakku' = layer cake (common at celebrations). Note the double 'k'
examples: Tein suklaakakun syntymäpäiväksi. — I made a chocolate cake for the birthday.

items.notes: Finns eat a lot of ice cream, even in winter. Note: 'jää' = ice, with double ä and ö at the end
examples: Haluatko jäätelöä? — Would you like ice cream?

items.notes: Finnish chocolate brand Fazer is very famous. 'Fazerin sininen' (Fazer Blue) is the iconic Finnish chocolate bar
examples: Rakastan suklaata. — I love chocolate.

items.notes: 'Sokeroimaton' = unsweetened, 'tomusokeri' = powdered sugar
examples: Otatko sokeria kahviin? — Do you take sugar in your coffee?

items.notes: 'Suolainen' = salty. Finnish salmiakki (salty liquorice) is a beloved national candy
examples: Lisää suolaa, kiitos. — More salt, please.

items.notes: Refers to black pepper. 'Paprika' = bell pepper (the vegetable)
examples: Haluatko pippuria? — Would you like pepper?

items.notes: A somewhat formal word for meal. In everyday speech, Finns might just say 'ruoka' (food)
examples: Ateria sisältää alkuruoan ja pääruoan. — The meal includes a starter and a main course.

items.notes: From 'aamu' (morning). Finnish breakfast typically includes bread, porridge, coffee, and dairy
examples: Aamiainen on kello seitsemältä. — Breakfast is at seven o'clock.

items.notes: Finnish lunch is typically eaten between 11:00-12:00. Many workplaces have subsidized lunches
examples: Mennään lounaalle! — Let's go for lunch!

items.notes: From 'päivä' (day). Dinner is eaten earlier in Finland, typically around 17:00-18:00
examples: Mitä on päivälliseksi? — What's for dinner?

items.notes: Literally 'between-piece' (väli = between, pala = piece). A small meal between main meals
examples: Otin pienen välipalan. — I had a small snack.

items.notes: Used for both plates and dishes. 'Lautasliina' = napkin (lit: plate cloth)
examples: Laita ruoka lautaselle. — Put the food on the plate.

items.notes: Means both a drinking glass and glass (material). 'Vesilasi' = water glass
examples: Anna minulle lasi vettä. — Give me a glass of water.

items.notes: 'Kahvikuppi' = coffee cup. 'Kupillinen' = a cupful
examples: Juon kaksi kuppia kahvia päivässä. — I drink two cups of coffee per day.
